text:
AccountingEquivalences
1. The recording of these events must follow theaccounting equation.
2. Doubleentrysystemsstatesthateveryaccountingeventaffectsat leasttwoaccounts,
keepingtheequationin balance.
There are two types of events depending on their effect on the company’s Net Equity:
➢Even transactions: they produce changes in the internal composition of the balance
sheet, but they do not alter net equity.
➢Uneven transactions: they affect net equity.
Depending on thetransaction, the company may report the event in its balance sheet
under assets and liabilities or in its income statement under revenues and expenses,
or in both of them.
+ Assets= -Assets
+ Assets= + Liabilities
-Liabilities= -Assets
-Liabilities= + Liabilities
+ Asset= + Net Equity
-Liabilities= + Net Equity
-Net Equity= -Assets
-Net Equity= + Liabilities

Increase in ASSETS = Increase in LIABILITIES
✓To get a new bank credit
✓To buy equipment on credit
Decrease in LIABILITIES = Decrease in ASSETS
✓To pay the outstanding debt with a supplier
✓To repay a loan
Decrease in LIABILITIES = Increase in LIABILITIES
✓To extend a debt due date more than 1 year
✓To change the nature of a debt (From suppliers to Notes Payable)

1 Expense/Loss (Decrease NE) = Decrease in ASSETS
✓To pay a rent in cash.
✓A machine being stolen.
2 Expense/Loss (Decrease NE) = Increase in LIABILITIES
✓Reception of the car rental invoice (no payment).
✓Reception of a Tax Authorities penalty (no
payment).
3 Increase in ASSETS = Revenue/Profit (Increase NE)
✓A service rendered to a client on credit.
✓Official grant conceded over a piece of land.
4 Decrease LIABILITIES = Revenues/Profit (Increase NE)
✓A debt with a third party is forgiven.
✓A year-end rappel is received from a supplier.
UNEVEN EVENTS. Some examples
1.Our company provideslegal advice and will be paid in 30
days
2.Our company purchases machinery on account
3.Our company collects the debt from our client
4.Our company receives interest in our bank account
5.A piece of land is granted to us as a subsidy
6.A supplier forgives our debt
7.Our company borrows money from the bank
8.Our company receives the end-of-year rebate on
purchases, it is deducted from our debt to the supplier

The recording of any accounting event must follow theaccounting equation:
Doubleentrysystemsstatesthateveryaccountingeventaffectsat leasttwo
accounts, keepingtheequationin balance.
